description: Convention Detection Engine - Trait-based pipeline with 8 pluggable detectors that analyze parsed IR to discover coding conventions, assign confidence scores, and cross-reference with documentation

userStories.title: ConventionDetector trait and detection pipeline with confidence scoring
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a trait-based detection pipeline that runs all detectors on parsed IR so that adding new detectors requires no changes to core scanning logic.
acceptanceCriteria: ConventionDetector trait defined in crates/seshat-detectors/src/trait_def.rs with name(), detect(), and supported_languages() methods
run_all_detectors(files, config) function orchestrates all registered detectors
Files processed in parallel via rayon::par_iter() (ADR-6), detectors run sequentially per file
Only detectors whose supported_languages() includes the file's language are executed
A failing detector logs tracing::warn and is skipped for that file — does not crash the pipeline
confidence.rs implements frequency-based scoring: adoption_count / total_count (ADR-7)
aggregate_findings groups findings by detector_name + description, computes adoption counts
Configurable weight thresholds from DetectionConfig: >0.85 Strong, 0.50-0.85 Moderate, 0.20-0.50 Weak, <0.20 Info
all_detectors() returns Vec<Box<dyn ConventionDetector>> with all detectors registered (initially empty or placeholder)
rayon added to seshat-detectors/Cargo.toml dependencies
Unit test: pipeline runs on empty file list without error
Unit test: failing detector is skipped, other detectors still run
Unit test: confidence scoring produces correct weight mapping at boundaries
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Dependency usage detector — canonical libraries per domain
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect canonical libraries per domain so that AI agents use the right libraries.
acceptanceCriteria: dependency_usage.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages (Rust, TypeScript, JavaScript, Python)
Groups DependencyUsage entries from IR by domain (HTTP, logging, testing, validation, serialization, database, CLI, async runtime)
Domain classification uses a curated mapping of known crate/package names to domains
Most-used library per domain identified as canonical (highest import count across files)
Conflicting libraries for the same domain flagged as Observation findings
Dead dependencies (declared in manifest but never imported in code) flagged
Each finding includes CodeEvidence with representative import lines
Unit tests with fixture files in tests/fixtures/ for at least Rust and TypeScript
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Import organization detector — grouping and ordering patterns
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect import grouping and ordering patterns so that AI agents follow the project's import style.
acceptanceCriteria: imports.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Detects grouping pattern: stdlib then external then internal (with blank line separators)
Detects presence/absence of consistent grouping across files
Rust-specific: use statement grouping (std/core then external crates then crate/super/self)
TypeScript/JavaScript-specific: barrel vs direct import preference, type-only import separation (import type)
Python-specific: import vs from-import preference, isort-style grouping
Each finding includes CodeEvidence with actual import blocks showing the detected pattern
follows_convention bool correctly set per file (does this file follow the majority pattern?)
Unit tests per language with fixture sample files (import_grouping.rs, import_patterns.ts, import_grouping.py)
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Error handling detector — error types, propagation, wrapping
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect error handling patterns so that AI agents use consistent error handling.
acceptanceCriteria: error_handling.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Rust: detects thiserror vs anyhow vs custom error enums; detects ? propagation; detects error wrapping patterns (map_err, context)
TypeScript: detects custom error classes vs plain Error; detects try-catch patterns; detects Result/Either patterns if used
JavaScript: detects error handling style (try-catch, callback errors, Promise rejection)
Python: detects exception hierarchy (custom vs built-in); detects try-except patterns; detects error wrapping
Findings include code examples of the dominant error handling pattern
Uses RustIR.error_types field from parsed IR where available
Unit tests per language using existing fixture files (thiserror_errors.rs, custom_errors.ts)
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Naming conventions detector — case patterns for files, functions, types, constants
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect naming conventions so that AI agents follow consistent naming.
acceptanceCriteria: naming.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Detects naming conventions for: files, functions, types/classes, constants, variables (where extractable from IR)
Analyzes function names from ProjectFile.functions, type names from ProjectFile.types
Detects case patterns: snake_case, camelCase, PascalCase, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE, kebab-case (files)
Language-aware weighting: Rust conventions weighted lower (compiler enforces them); JS/Python/TS weighted higher
Findings describe the dominant pattern with adoption percentage
Unit tests with mixed-style fixture files
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Export patterns detector — default vs named, barrel exports, pub/mod
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect export patterns so that AI agents create consistent module boundaries.
acceptanceCriteria: exports.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
TypeScript/JavaScript: detects default vs named export preference with adoption rate
TypeScript/JavaScript: detects barrel export pattern (re-exports from index.ts/index.js) via TypeScriptIR.has_barrel_exports or file path check
Rust: detects pub usage patterns, mod re-export patterns
Python: detects __all__ usage pattern via PythonIR.has_all_export
Each finding includes representative code evidence
Unit tests using existing fixture files (barrel_exports.ts)
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Logging and observability detector — library and structured vs unstructured preference
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect logging patterns so that AI agents use the right logging library and format.
acceptanceCriteria: logging.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Identifies canonical logging library (tracing vs log for Rust, winston vs pino for JS/TS, logging vs loguru for Python)
Detects structured vs unstructured logging preference (structured = fields/key-value, unstructured = string interpolation)
Conflicting logging libraries flagged as Observation
Uses DependencyUsage from IR to identify which logging library is imported in each file
Unit tests using existing fixture files (tracing_logging.rs, logging_patterns.py)
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Test patterns detector — framework, file placement, naming conventions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect testing conventions so that AI agents write tests matching project style.
acceptanceCriteria: tests_pattern.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Identifies testing framework (Rust built-in #[test], Jest, Mocha, pytest, unittest)
Detects test file placement: co-located (mod tests in same file for Rust, *.test.ts next to source) vs separate (tests/ directory)
Detects test naming convention (test_*, it('should ...'), describe/it, def test_*)
Detects setup/teardown patterns (before/after hooks, fixtures, test builders)
Uses DependencyUsage to identify test framework imports
Unit tests using existing fixture files (jest_tests.ts, pytest_patterns.py)
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: File structure detector — directory organization patterns
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to detect file organization patterns so that AI agents place new files correctly.
acceptanceCriteria: file_structure.rs implements ConventionDetector for all 4 languages
Detects directory organization pattern: by feature (users/, orders/), by type (models/, controllers/, services/), by layer (domain/, infrastructure/, application/)
Identifies common directory conventions (src/, lib/, tests/, utils/, helpers/, types/)
Detects configuration file placement patterns (root vs config directory)
Analysis based on ProjectFile.path patterns across the entire file set, not individual file content
Documents chosen approach for handling per-file trait method vs project-wide analysis (interior mutability, separate aggregation pass, or context injection)
Unit tests with fixture directory structures
cargo test -p seshat-detectors passes
cargo clippy -p seshat-detectors -- -D warnings passes
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Chose per-file path classification with pipeline aggregation — each detect() call classifies path components into org patterns (by-type, by-layer, by-feature), common dirs, and config placement. The existing aggregate_findings() groups these per-file findings by description to produce project-wide totals. No trait changes needed.

userStories.title: Cross-reference code conventions vs documentation
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Seshat to compare code conventions with documentation so that contradictions are surfaced.
acceptanceCriteria: cross_reference.rs implemented (in seshat-detectors or seshat-graph depending on DB access needs — decide and document)
Loads code-detected conventions (Nature = Convention/Observation) and documentation-sourced knowledge nodes (Nature = Fact/Rule)
Compares via keyword/topic matching: doc node description vs convention description (no semantic/NLP — ADR-23)
Matching conventions: confidence boosted (reinforcement)
Contradictions: Contradicts edge created between the doc node and code convention node
Unit test: doc says 'use X', code convention says 'use Y' produces Contradicts edge
Unit test: doc says 'use X', code convention confirms X produces confidence boost
cargo test passes for affected crate(s)
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes for affected crate(s)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: ADR-23 places cross-reference in seshat-graph/src/cross_reference.rs. If it needs storage access, it should live in seshat-graph. If pure in-memory, it could live in seshat-detectors.

userStories.title: Branch code review and quality gate
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a comprehensive code review of all changes in the feature branch before merging so that the code meets professional Rust quality standards.
acceptanceCriteria: cargo fmt --all --check passes with no formatting violations
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings passes with no lint warnings
cargo test --workspace passes including all integration tests
cargo doc --no-deps --document-private-items builds without warnings
No new unsafe blocks added without justification
No .unwrap() or .expect() in library code (only in tests)
Error handling uses thiserror for error types and ? for propagation
No unnecessary allocations: &str over String in params, &[T] over Vec<T> where ownership not needed
No needless .clone() — each clone verified as necessary
Collections pre-allocated with with_capacity() where size is known
No copy-pasted logic between detectors — shared patterns extracted into helper functions
Confidence scoring logic centralized in confidence.rs, not reimplemented per detector
All code within seshat-detectors crate boundary (no SQL, no MCP, no CLI code)
Only depends on seshat-core (and rayon, tracing, thiserror as external deps)
Follows established patterns from seshat-core and seshat-scanner
Tests are deterministic with descriptive names and edge cases covered
Doc comments on all public items; module-level doc comments on each module
Full git diff main...HEAD review passes quality gate
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is a review/quality gate story, not a code-writing story. Run all automated checks and review the full diff.
